title: Enhanced qubit performance by integrating altermagnets into superconducting qubit designs
authors: Johanne Bratland Tjernshaugen, Morten Amundsen, Jacob Linder
abstract: Identifying a materials platform for creating qubits that are both tunable and resilient towards environmental noise is one of the main hurdles that need to be overcome to realize quantum computation that is practically useful. One pursued avenue to this end is to use superconducting qubits with intrinsic spin-dependent interactions, such as spin-orbit coupling or magnetism. However, the recently discovered class of materials known as altermagnets remain largely unexplored in this context. We here use microscopic calculations to determine how the properties of superconducting qubits are modified when altermagnetic Josephson junctions are included. The key qubit performance parameters, including splitting, anharmonicity, decoherence, and single/coupled-qubit gate operation times, display rich behavior depending on the characteristic properties of the altermagnetic material, such as the strength of the Néel field and the crystallographic orientation of the altermagnetic relative interfaces in the system. We focus in particular on the transmon design and show that the qubit is very well protected against decoherence and simultaneously shows superior anharmonicity both near 0-$π$ transition points and when it is in a $φ$-state. We propose that by using strain, the altermagnetic qubit can be moved out of its protected regime to enable faster gate-operation times, and then moved back to its protected state. We also discuss how the altermagnetic properties influence flux qubits and fluxonium. Our results suggest that integration of altermagnetic materials into existing superconducting qubit design can substantially improve their performance due to the unique properties of the altermagnetic band-structure.
summary: This theoretical study proposes using altermagnets to enhance superconducting qubits by modifying the Josephson junction properties. By calculating the resulting energy-phase relation, the authors show that these junctions can yield qubits with superior anharmonicity and reduced decoherence. This integration offers a pathway to building more robust and controllable quantum processors.
main_problem: The primary hurdle in realizing practical quantum computation is finding qubit materials that are both tunable and resilient to environmental noise.
main_result: Integrating altermagnets into superconducting qubits significantly improves performance by offering superior anharmonicity and robust protection against decoherence near specific operational regimes.
method: The study uses microscopic calculations, generalized Blonder-Tinkham-Klapwijk (BTK) theory, and Hamiltonian analysis to model the energy-phase relation of altermagnetic Josephson junctions.
model_or_system: The system involves superconducting qubits, particularly the transmon design, interfaced with altermagnetic Josephson junctions. The analysis models the resulting Josephson potential $U_{AM}(\varphi)$ and the full qubit Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Qubit splitting, anharmonicity ($\alpha$), decoherence rates ($\Gamma_1, \Gamma_\varphi$), and single/coupled-qubit gate operation times ($T_\pi$).
important_parameters: Strength of the Néel field, crystallographic orientation ($\gamma$), and the superconducting phase difference ($\varphi$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on approximations like truncating the energy-phase relation to a Fourier series and assuming zero temperature for some free energy calculations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the Josephson potential $U_{AM}(\varphi)$ dependence on altermagnet strength, the harmonic content of the potential, and the resulting decoherence rates and $\pi$-pulse times as a function of material parameters.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the need for new qubit materials, deriving the Josephson energy via microscopic calculations, analyzing the resulting potential features ($\phi$-states, $0-\pi$ transitions), and finally applying these findings to calculate key performance metrics like decoherence and gate times for various qubit types.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the materials science challenge in quantum computing by proposing a novel magnetic material class (altermagnets) to engineer superconducting qubit parameters, which is highly relevant to open quantum systems and superconducting circuit QED.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Properties of the altermagnetic potential for 𝛾= 0 (left column) and 𝛾= 𝜋/4 (right column). (a) and (b) show the harmonic content of the potential 𝑈AM(𝜑) = −Í 𝑛𝐸𝑛cos(𝑛𝜑). The coefficients 𝐸𝑛were found using the method of least squares on the raw data, and the figure shows the first three components. (c) and (d) shows the phase value 𝜑0 at the minima of 𝑈AM for 𝜑∈[0, 𝜋]. The inset in (c) shows the potential profile at 𝑡0 ≈0.16, and this 𝑡0 strength is marked with yellow dashed lines in (a) and (c).

FIG. 1. Altermagnetic Josephson potential 𝑈AM(𝜑) as a function of the superconducting phase 𝜑and the altermagnetic strength 𝑡0 for (a) pure 𝑑𝑥𝑦altermagnetism and (b) pure 𝑑𝑥2−𝑦2 altermagnetism. The orange dashed lines are visual guides for the 0 −𝜋transitions upon increasing 𝑡0. We have subtracted the minimum value of 𝑈AM. The parameters used are the junction length 𝑑= 0.2𝜉and width 𝑊= 10𝑑 with 𝜉the superconducting coherence length, the interface parameter 𝑍= 0.4, the superconducting gap Δ = 0.15 meV, and the Fermi wave vector 𝑘𝐹𝜉= 100.

FIG. 3. The location of the Josephson potential minima depends on the length 𝑑and 𝑡0 in the 𝛾= 0 configuration. When 𝜑0 ≠{0, 𝜋}, the junction is in a 𝜙-state. We used the analytical expressions in [14] with transmission probability Γ = 0.86.

FIG. 4. We focus on a transmon qubit design where an altermagnetic Josephson junction is parallel-coupled to a capacitor. The JJ has a free energy 𝑈AM(𝜑) and we consider two crystallographic orientations of the altermagnetic lattice relative the interfaces to the superconductors, depicted in the figure.

FIG. 5. Qubit frequency 𝜔(blue) and anharmonicity 𝛼(gray) for (a) 𝛾= 0 and (b) 𝛾= 𝜋/4. The horizontal dashed lines represent the 𝑡0 →0 values, while the orange dotted lines are visual guides for the 0 −𝜋transitions in the altermagnetic potential. We set 𝐸𝑐/ℎ= 0.25 GHz, which is a typical experimental value [28].
